summary: The past 30 sessions show a burst-clustering pattern: multiple short sessions open in rapid succession on the same day (e.g. 5 sessions within 2 hours on 2026-05-14), suggesting iterative debugging or feature-spike work rather than single sustained sessions. Session cadence is uneven — heavy days cluster around May 8, 11–12, and 14–16, with gaps in between, pointing to project-sprint rhythms rather than daily steady work. No clear evidence of abandoned sessions from metadata alone, though the high density of same-day multi-session starts may indicate sessions that ended early and were restarted.

insights.name: same-day session burst clustering
insights.instruction: Watch for sessions that open within 10–30 minutes of a prior session close on the same project — these are likely context-loss restarts. Cross-reference with /core-dump and /catchup usage to see if checkpoint discipline is holding.

insights.name: sprint-gap-sprint cadence across project days
insights.instruction: Session density spikes on ~3-4 active days per week with 1-2 day gaps. If a project memory or checkpoint was written before the gap, verify it's being loaded at the start of the next sprint day via /catchup.

insights.name: mid-day session concentration
insights.instruction: Most sessions start between 11:00–16:00 UTC. If sessions consistently end before context is serialized (no /core-dump), runtime-notes and WAL entries from these sessions may be incomplete. Watch for sessions that span less than 30 minutes — those are the highest-risk for orphaned context.

The burst-restart pattern correlates with the user's known heavy /catchup and /core-dump reliance (see feedback_session_continuity.md). Bursts that don't use /catchup at session open are the gap to close.
